lightBloom.note: The current price line should have a small bloom that flashes on each tick update — the price is ALIVE, always moving
Green P&L numbers should have very subtle bloom — money glowing
MARGIN CALL WARNING should bloom red — this is the 'Margin Call' movie moment

chromaticAberration.note: None needed. Bloomberg terminals are flat-panel precision displays. Clean image is the aesthetic

filmGrain.note: 0.01 at 24fps is very subtle. This is correct for Bloomberg. But add a unique Bloomberg artifact: CRT phosphor pattern (specified as rgb-stripe at 0.08). This should be visible on close inspection — the RGB sub-pixel grid of an old Bloomberg terminal

priceFlash.note: Uptick/downtick flash is specified at 8-frame duration. Enhance: uptick flash should be a rapid GREEN -> WHITE -> GREEN transition over 4 frames, downtick RED -> WHITE -> RED. The white flash at frame 2 sells the 'tick just happened' immediacy

scanLines.note: Spacing 2 at 0.03. Add HORIZONTAL BANDING — every 180px, a slightly brighter band (0.02 opacity increase) that slowly scrolls upward at 0.1px/frame. Old CRT refresh artifact

reflectionGlare.note: Multiple monitors on a desk create cross-reflections. Add very faint rectangular highlights from adjacent monitors — suggests this is one of 6 screens

motionBlur.note: Ticker tape should have distinct horizontal motion blur — it scrolls fast enough to smear
When order book levels shift, brief vertical motion blur on the moving rows — the ladder slides

cameraDrift.note: Near-zero. Trading terminals are on desks. But add occasional deliberate 'lean-in' — every 120 frames, scale increases 100.5% for 15 frames then returns, as if the trader leans toward the screen

dramaticReveals.note: Terminal boot at frames 0-40 is good. Add the classic Bloomberg login: orange text on black, 'BLOOMBERG PROFESSIONAL SERVICE', progress bar, then panels populate in Z-pattern (top-left first). Boot text should include 'CONNECTING TO MARKET DATA...OK'
Cell reveal at frames 50-70 is specified. Each cell should fade in with its color intensifying from gray to final correlation color — like a heatmap warming up

breathingAnimations.note: Order book sizes should CONSTANTLY change — flicker between values every 4-8 frames. Real order books are never still. This is the single most important animation for authenticity
The rightmost candle (current) should have its high/low wicks constantly extending and retracting — the price is being discovered in real-time
VaR numbers should slowly recalculate — digits roll through intermediate values to a new settled value every 60 frames

transitionMoments.description: Alert cascade — MARGIN CALL warning slides in (frame 80), immediately followed by VOLATILITY SPIKE (frame 85). Order book spread widens from 0.08 to 0.16. Candlestick current candle extends downward sharply. P&L ticker values flash red. RSI drops from 62 to 48. The room feels the tremor
Recovery rally — price reverses upward. New candle forms green. Order book bid side thickens. P&L ticks back up. VIX equivalent decreases. Correlation matrix subtly shifts. The tension breaks

dataDensityGaps.fix: Add VWAP deviation bands (1 and 2 sigma), time-weighted average price line, and a miniature volume-at-price histogram on the right edge (specified as separate element but should be integrated into the chart panel)
Add LAST TRADE indicator — a flashing arrow (up/down) next to the mid-price showing the direction of the last execution. Add IMBALANCE RATIO trend (last 30 readings as a mini-sparkline)
Add P&L ATTRIBUTION breakdown: how much P&L is from alpha (skill) vs beta (market). Add POSITION CONCENTRATION risk — largest single-name exposure as % of portfolio
Trade blotter and bloomberg header are thin. Add a TIME AND SALES panel with a speed histogram showing trade velocity in 1-second bins — how fast the tape is moving
Add UNREALIZED vs REALIZED P&L split, HOLDING PERIOD per position, and a RED FLAG column for positions exceeding risk limits
Add a dashed-line ring showing TARGET allocation overlaid on actual — visual delta between where you are and where you should be

colorGradingNotes.overallLook: Near-monochrome with green/red punches — grade like Margin Call (J.C. Chandor). The Bloomberg orange (#FF8800) is the only brand color; everything else is black, gray, green, or red. Shadows should be lifted slightly to #111111, never pure black. The overall tone should feel CLINICAL, like fluorescent office lighting — slightly desaturated, slightly too cold

keyMoments.shift: Subtle red wash across the entire screen — 0.02 opacity #FF1133 overlay for 30 frames. The trader's blood pressure just spiked
Shift lifts — very slight green tint to the background for 20 frames. Relief
Bloomberg orange (#FF8800) dominates during boot — black screen with orange text. Then the color drains to the normal muted palette as panels populate

colorGradingNotes.accentUsage: Green/red for up/down is universal and well-applied. The Bloomberg orange needs more presence — it appears in the header and top banner border but should also accent the chart timeframe selector and active panel headers
colorGradingNotes.backgroundAtmosphere: Background is appropriately near-black. Add very subtle horizontal banding at 0.01 opacity — the CRT RGB stripe pattern creates barely visible lines that say 'this is a terminal, not a webpage'

typographyNotes.fonts: Consolas is the authentic Bloomberg terminal font — perfect choice. Inter for secondary text is a good modern complement
typographyNotes.monospaceReadout: Consolas at weight 400 for data is correct. For prices, use weight 700 — prices must stand out from surrounding data. Ensure TABULAR FIGURES everywhere — alignment is sacred in financial data
typographyNotes.displayFont: Ticker price at 32px is good. But the correlation matrix cell values at 11px mono are too small — bump to 13px for the diagonal (1.00) values which are reference points
typographyNotes.sizeHierarchy: 9px labels are standard Bloomberg density — acceptable for experienced users. 11px table cells are fine. Add a 'flash' tier: when a price updates, briefly render at 2px larger then snap back — visual tick indicator

continuousAmbience.sound: Rapid soft clicking — like a stock ticker mechanical tape. This is the sound of price discovery
Faint electronic hum with rhythmic pulse — the tape never stops
Trading floor ambient — distant keyboards, muffled phone conversations, occasional sharp 'FILL!' shout from a distant desk

eventSounds.sound: Sharp three-tone descending alarm (G5-D5-Bb4) — unmistakable danger. This sound should make the viewer's stomach drop
Soft mechanical snap — the timeframe bar closing
Brief electronic pip — pitched higher for upticks, lower for downticks. Frequency of pips indicates market velocity
Deep resonant bell — crossing from profit to loss or vice versa is a significant event

silenceMoments.description: The split-second before the margin call alert — the order book briefly freezes (sizes stop flickering for 5 frames). Something is wrong. Then the alert hits
